description: Figure 3 displays a bar-and-error-bar plot of regression coefficients r_i (in GPa) quantifying the effect of individual components on the elastic modulus of austenitic steels, ordered by decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i). Visual data points with error bars (standard deviations) are shown for Fe (~2.0 GPa), Cr (~2.4 GPa), Ni (~1.9 GPa), NbC (~6.5 GPa), M23C6 (Cr23C6, ~1.9 GPa), TiC (~7.3 GPa), interstitial N (~15.1 GPa with the largest uncertainty), and Mo (~0.5 GPa). While all plotted coefficients appear positive, caption states that Ni and Mo decrease the elastic modulus, whereas N, NbC, TiC, and Cr increase it; M23C6 is reported as statistically significant yet effectively negligible. The analysis covers 24 steel batches with austenite compositions and carbide phase contents calculated using Thermo-Calc. The regression model yields R² = 0.83, a standard error of 2.9 GPa, a maximum deviation of 3.1 GPa, and an average deviation of 1.1 GPa. The figure demonstrates that carbide phases and nitrogen exert the strongest positive influences, although N suffers from low significance due to limited alloy batches.
